desc: Medically significant — the only dangerous spider regularly encountered in LA County. Glossy black with red hourglass on ventral abdomen. Webs in dark, undisturbed areas.

fm.Size: ♀: ⅓ in–⅔ in body. ♂: much smaller.
fm.Web: Irregular, strong cobweb in sheltered dark areas
fm.Eyes: 8 eyes; poor vision; relies on vibrations
fm.Habitat: Under rocks, woodpiles, outdoor structures, garages
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; shy; bites only when trapped against skin
fm.Season: Year-round; males wander seeking mates in spring–fall

hp: Builds irregular cobwebs in dark sheltered spaces — garages, woodpiles, meter boxes. Spider associate.

desc: Invasive — arrived in SoCal ~2000s, now abundant. Tan-brown with geometric pattern. Spiky egg sac diagnostic. Less venomous than Black Widow but rapidly displacing it.

fm.Web: Irregular cobweb under outdoor furniture, eaves, cars
fm.Habitat: Urban — under patio furniture, mailboxes, playground equipment
fm.Behavior: Less aggressive than Black Widow; frequently encountered

hp: Builds irregular cobwebs in dark sheltered spaces — garages, woodpiles, meter boxes. Invasive.

desc: Dark cobweb spider commonly confused with the true black widow (Latrodectus). Bulbous abdomen but lacks the diagnostic red hourglass marking. Harmless. Actually preys on black widows when the two species encounter each other. Beneficial household spider.

fm.Web: Cobweb in dark corners, garages, basements
fm.Behavior: Shy; bite causes mild, brief pain only

hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. Preys on black widow spiders (Latrodectus).

desc: Largest and most conspicuous jumping spider in LA. Bright red abdomen contrasts with black cephalothorax. Active visual hunter with charismatic behavior.

fm.Web: No hunting web; silken retreat for resting/molting
fm.Habitat: Open sunny areas, fences, walls, garden structures
fm.Behavior: Active diurnal hunter; curious; turns to face observers

hp: Red-backed jumping spider — active visual hunter on shrubs and walls. Preys on flies, moths, other spiders. Curious — investigates humans.

desc: Black with white spots and brilliant iridescent green chelicerae. One of the most commonly encountered and recognized spiders in North America. Active diurnal hunter with exceptional vision — can track prey, recognize threats, and plan routes. Common in gardens.

fm.Eyes: 4 large anterior eyes; excellent binocular vision
fm.Habitat: Gardens, buildings, fences — any sunny surface
fm.Behavior: Active hunter; stalks prey with cat-like approach

hp: Most common jumping spider in NA (Edwards 2004). Curious visual hunter approaching humans. Preys on flies, moths, other spiders. In gardens.

desc: Introduced tropical salticid now abundant in urban LA. Males distinctive with white lateral stripes on black body and central white band. On buildings and walls. Nocturnal as well as diurnal — unusual for jumpers. Synanthropic — thrives near humans.

fm.Habitat: Buildings, walls, window frames; strongly synanthropic
fm.Behavior: Active diurnal hunter; very fast

hp: Visual ambush hunter with acute eyesight. Stalks prey on vegetation by day.

desc: Large, spectacular orb weaver. Female builds web up to 2 feet across with distinctive zigzag stabilimentum in center.

fm.Size: ♀: ⅔ in–1 in body (very large). ♂: ⅛ in–⅓ in.
fm.Web: Large circular orb with thick zigzag stabilimentum
fm.Habitat: Gardens, meadows, tall vegetation in sunny areas
fm.Behavior: Sits head-down in web center; bounces web to capture prey

hp: Large orb web in gardens — captures grasshoppers, flies, bees. Stabilimentum (zigzag silk). Prey for mud dauber wasps and birds.

desc: Silvery orb weaver with distinctively lobed abdomen. Web includes conspicuous stabilimentum (zigzag silk band). Common in warm SoCal gardens. Smaller than A. aurantia but more silvery. Sits head-down in web center with legs in X-pattern.

fm.Web: Orb web with X or cross-shaped stabilimentum
fm.Habitat: Gardens, warm dry areas, coastal scrub
fm.Behavior: Sits in web center with legs in X-shape

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In gardens. In gardens and urban areas. Coastal zone.

desc: The most common large orb weaver in LA gardens. Builds new web nightly in the same location. Variable coloring.

fm.Web: Vertical orb web; rebuilt nightly; sits in center at night
fm.Habitat: Gardens, under eaves, near outdoor lights (attracts prey)
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal web builder; hides during day; attracted to lights

hp: Most common orb-weaving spider in SoCal gardens. Builds and dismantles web nightly. Preys on moths, flies. Highly variable color/pattern.

desc: Large, fast spider frequently found indoors. Builds funnel-shaped sheet web. Males wander seeking females in fall.

fm.Size: ♀: ½ in–⅔ in body; leg span up to 3 in
fm.Web: Flat sheet web with funnel retreat in corners
fm.Habitat: Indoors — garages, basements, closets, window wells
fm.Behavior: Fast runner when disturbed; males wander in fall

hp: Giant house spider — funnel web in garages and basements. Preys on insects and other spiders. Males wander indoors seeking mates in fall.

desc: Large, flat-bodied spider with legs spread crab-like. Nocturnal ambush predator on walls and ceilings. Harmless.

fm.Size: ♀: ⅔ in–¾ in body; very large leg span
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, rocky areas; sometimes enters homes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flat against surfaces; extremely fast

hp: Predator of insects and other arthropods. Nocturnal. Flattened body.

desc: One of the largest wolf spiders. Females carry egg sac on spinnerets, then carry spiderlings on back. Ground-dwelling nocturnal hunter.

fm.Web: No web; digs shallow burrow or roams
fm.Eyes: 8 eyes; 2 large posterior median eyes reflective at night
fm.Habitat: Open ground, gardens, under debris; burrows in soil
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; active hunter; ♀ carries egg sac then spiderlings on back

hp: Wolf spider — ground-running nocturnal hunter. Pursues prey. Female carries egg sac on spinnerets.

desc: Ambush predator that changes color between white and yellow to match flower color. Catches pollinators on blooms.

fm.Web: No web; ambush predator on flowers
fm.Habitat: On flowers — especially composites, roses, buckwheat
fm.Behavior: Color-changing ambush predator; catches bees and butterflies on flowers

hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. Predatory.

desc: Longbodied cellar spider — the familiar 'daddy longlegs' of houses and garages. Extremely common indoors worldwide. Vibrates web rapidly when disturbed ('vibrating spider'). Preys on other spiders including black widows. Long-legged but tiny-bodied. European introduction.

fm.Size: ♀: ¼ in–⅓ in body; very long legs (1–2 in)
fm.Web: Loose irregular web in corners, ceilings, garages
fm.Eyes: 8 eyes in two groups of three + two
fm.Behavior: Vibrates rapidly when disturbed ("vibrating spider"); preys on other spiders

hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. Preys on black widow spiders (Latrodectus).

desc: Introduced. Brick-red cephalothorax and large forward-pointing fangs specialized for piercing isopod (sowbug) armor. Found under logs and bark.

fm.Web: Silken retreat under bark/logs; no prey web
fm.Habitat: Under bark, logs, rocks, leaf litter — wherever sowbugs live
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal sowbug specialist; large fangs pierce isopod armor

hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Under bark. Large species.

desc: LA County's native tarantula. Males wander in fall seeking mates — the famous "tarantula migration." Docile, long-lived (♀ 20+ years). Burrow dweller.

fm.Size: ♀: body 1.5–2 in; leg span up to 5 in
fm.Web: Lines burrow with silk; no prey web
fm.Eyes: 8 tiny eyes; poor vision; relies on vibration
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, grasslands, oak woodland; hillside burrows
fm.Behavior: ♂ wander Sep–Nov seeking ♀; docile; flicks urticating hairs if harassed

hp: Burrowing species.

desc: Native to SoCal deserts — the only recluse spider established in the LA region. Medically significant bite (necrotic). Violin marking on cephalothorax; 6 eyes (not 8).

fm.Web: Irregular sheet web in dark recesses
fm.Eyes: 6 eyes in 3 pairs (diagnostic — most spiders have 8)
fm.Habitat: Desert and semi-arid areas; rock crevices; occasionally buildings
fm.Behavior: Extremely shy; nocturnal; bites only when pressed against skin

hp: In dark undisturbed spaces. Preys on small insects. Necrotic venom potential. Not established outdoors in LA. Desert specialist.

desc: Native SoCal trapdoor spider. Builds silk-lined burrow with hinged lid camouflaged with debris. Ambush predator.

fm.Web: Silk-lined burrow with hinged trapdoor lid
fm.Behavior: Sits in burrow, lid slightly open; lunges at passing prey

hp: Burrow-dwelling ambush predator. Waits at entrance for passing prey. Long-lived.

desc: The largest scorpion in North America — up to 6 in. Desert-edge LA County. Fluoresces brilliant green under UV light. Sting painful but not medically significant.

fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, sandy washes — northeast LA County
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; ambush predator; fluoresces green under UV

hp: Largest NA scorpion (to 140mm; Sissom 1990). Desert burrow ambusher. Mild venom — less dangerous than Centruroides.

desc: The most commonly encountered scorpion in residential SoCal. Small, tan, found under rocks and in homes. Mild sting.

fm.Habitat: Under rocks, bark, debris; enters homes; gardens
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hides in moist dark areas; UV-detectable

hp: Most common scorpion in LA residential areas. Burrows under rocks and debris. Mild sting. Preys on insects. UV-fluorescent exoskeleton.

desc: Bright green ambush predator on flowers. Female guards egg sac aggressively and can spit venom up to 20cm. Important natural enemy of crop pests. Common on garden flowers.

fm.Behavior: Ambush; ♀ guards egg sac; spits venom

hp: Green lynx spider — ambushes pollinators on flower heads. Female guards egg sac, sprays venom defensively. Important predator on garden flowers.

desc: European garden spider — large orb weaver with distinctive white cross pattern on abdomen. Introduced from Europe. Builds large orb webs in gardens. Common in fall.

fm.Color: Brown-orange with white cross on abdomen

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In gardens. In gardens and urban areas. Large species.

desc: Large orb weaver with silver and dark zigzag banding. Builds large orb webs in tall grass and gardens. Stabilimentum (zigzag silk) in web center.

fm.Color: Silver with dark bands; zigzag stabilimentum

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. Important spider predator in food web.

desc: Pale yellow-green spider building silk retreats in curled leaves and corners. Nocturnal wandering hunter. Enters homes frequently. Mild bite causes temporary redness — one of the most commonly encountered indoor spiders.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hides in sac by day

hp: Most common spider biter in SoCal (Vetter & Isbister 2008, Med J Aust). Nocturnal silk-retreat hunter. Mild local reaction. Yellow-green.

desc: Dark fast-running ground spider with white dorsal stripe ('collar'). Nocturnal hunter under rocks and debris. Can bite but not dangerous.

fm.Color: Dark with white dorsal collar mark; very fast

hp: Nocturnal predator. Spider specialist. Spider associate.

desc: Long-legged spider with marbled abdomen. Introduced from Mediterranean. Builds messy webs in garages and building overhangs. Displacing native cellar spiders.

fm.Size: ⅓ in body; very long legs

hp: Marbled cellar spider — introduced pholcid in garages and eaves. Hangs inverted in web. Preys on flies, mosquitoes, and other spiders.

desc: Introduced relative of the brown recluse. Six eyes in three pairs (diagnostic of Loxosceles). Potentially necrotic bite but less virulent than L. reclusa. Occasionally found in imported goods and older buildings. Not established outdoors in LA.

fm.Web: Irregular sheet web in dark recesses
fm.Behavior: Very shy; nocturnal; medically significant bite (rare)

hp: In dark undisturbed spaces. Preys on small insects. Necrotic venom potential. Not established outdoors in LA. Introduced species.

desc: Introduced from Canary Islands, now spreading aggressively in SoCal. Dark with cream crescent marking. Bite can cause localized pain and inflammation. Displacing native species.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; bite mildly medically significant

hp: Cobwebs on fences, under eaves. Introduced — spreading aggressively, displacing native spiders.

desc: Gray wall jumper — flattened jumping spider of sun-warmed walls and buildings. Two prominent white side stripes on dark body. Pantropical species common in urban LA. Very approachable.

fm.Behavior: Hunts on warm walls; flattened for crack-hiding

hp: Gray wall jumper — diurnal visual hunter on sun-warmed buildings. Flat body enters crevices. Preys on flies, mosquitoes on walls.

desc: Large rusty-brown orb weaver with cruciate (cross-shaped) dark mark on abdomen. Builds enormous webs on buildings at night. Very common urban spider.

fm.Color: Rusty brown with dark cross-mark on abdomen

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In buildings. Urban habitat.

desc: Large bold jumper with bright red abdomen and black markings. Males display in elaborate courtship. On chaparral shrubs. CA near-endemic.

fm.Behavior: Active hunter; curious; red color distinctive

hp: Jumping spider — diurnal visual hunter. Stalks and pounces on prey. Preys on flies and small insects.

desc: The most commonly encountered tick on LA hiking trails. Three-host tick that feeds on lizards as larvae, rodents as nymphs, and large mammals as adults. Can transmit Rocky Mountain spotted fever and tularemia.

fm.Size: ⅛ in unfed; ½ in engorged
fm.Behavior: Quests on grass tips for passing hosts

hp: Pacific Coast tick — most common human-biting tick in CA. Vectors Rocky Mountain spotted fever, tularemia, 364D rickettsiosis.

desc: Grape jumping spider — tiny colorful jumper found on grape and other plants. ♂ with bright red-orange face and metallic green chelicerae. Elaborate courtship display.

fm.Color: ♂ red face, green chelicerae; ♀ brown striped

hp: On grape and garden plants. Tiny visual hunter — stalks prey on leaf surfaces. Colorful male display.

desc: Fire-faced paradise spider — strikingly colorful jumper with red face and green chelicerae in males. Elaborate courtship dance includes leg-waving, body vibrations, and color displays. Ground-dwelling.

fm.Behavior: ♂ elaborate courtship dance with colors; studied for vision

hp: Active ground hunter in chaparral. Males perform elaborate courtship with colorful face and leg displays.

desc: The most frequently encountered scorpion in LA County. Small tan scorpion with mild sting. Fluoresces bright green under UV light. Nocturnal — common under rocks.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; glows under UV; mild sting

hp: Nocturnal — attracted to lights. UV fluorescent — glows under blacklight.

desc: The most commonly encountered tarantula in LA County. Males cross roads and trails in fall seeking mates. Dark brown-black. Docile — rarely bites.

fm.Behavior: ♂ wander roads Oct–Nov; docile; urticating hairs

hp: Most common tarantula in LA foothills. Males wander roads in fall seeking mates. Females live 20-25 yrs. Preys on insects, other spiders.

desc: Primary Lyme disease vector on the West Coast. Smaller and darker than the Pacific Coast tick. Quests on vegetation for passing hosts. Peak activity in winter/spring.

fm.Behavior: Transmits Lyme disease; nymphs highest risk spring

hp: Ticks — ectoparasites. Blood-feeders. Disease vectors.

desc: Simon's hacklemesh spider — introduced South American spider now very common in SoCal. Builds fuzzy hackled-band webs on fences and walls. Fast runner. Dark brown. Often mistaken for brown recluse.

fm.Behavior: Very fast; fuzzy bluish web; harmless

hp: In dark undisturbed spaces. Preys on small insects. Necrotic venom potential. Not established outdoors in LA. Densely hairy.

desc: Pocoki's burrowing scorpion — large thick-tailed scorpion excavating deep burrows. Ambushes prey from burrow entrance. Thick metasoma (tail) diagnostic. Mild sting.
hp: Deep burrow excavator. Ambush predator at burrow entrance. Thick-tailed scorpion. Foothill habitats.

desc: Introduced Australian spider building distinctive lace-sheet webs on window frames, fences, and walls. Very common on building exteriors. Expanding in SoCal since ~1990s.

fm.Behavior: Builds lace-web; very common on buildings

hp: Grey house spider — Australian introduction. Lace-sheet webs on window frames and eaves. Preys on moths, flies attracted to lights.

desc: Western colonus — small dark jumping spider extremely common on buildings, fences, and garden walls. Active day-hunter with excellent vision. One of the most frequently seen spiders in urban LA.

fm.Behavior: Active hunter; common urban jumper

hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Gardens and urban areas.

desc: Remarkable color-changing ambush spider on flowers. Can slowly shift between white and yellow over days to match the flower it hunts on. Grabs pollinators visiting flowers.

fm.Behavior: Changes color white↔yellow to match flower; ambush predator

hp: White-banded crab spider — color-changing ambush predator on flowers. Captures bees, butterflies, flies visiting flowers. Prey for birds.

desc: Pacific broad-faced sac spider — dark reddish-brown spider with notably wide head. Nocturnal hunter under bark and debris. Can bite but not medically significant.

fm.Habitat: Under bark, leaf litter, enters homes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; broad cephalothorax distinctive

hp: Pacific broad-faced sac spider — nocturnal hunter under bark and in buildings. Preys on small insects. Can bite — mild local reaction.

desc: Extraordinary hunting method — swings sticky bolas ball on silk thread to catch moths. Lures with moth pheromone mimicry.

fm.Web: No orb — swings single sticky silk bolas
fm.Habitat: Shrubs, gardens — where moths fly at night
fm.Behavior: Swings sticky silk ball at moths; mimics moth pheromones; unique hunting strategy

hp: Mimicry — Batesian or aggressive. Mimicry specialist. Southern.

desc: The only scorpion in NA with potentially life-threatening sting. Climbs walls. Rare in LA County — desert edge.

fm.Habitat: Desert — under bark, in rock crevices; climbs walls/ceilings
fm.Behavior: Climbs vertical surfaces; bark-loving; most medically significant NA scorpion

hp: Desert scorpion — ambush predator at burrow entrance.

desc: Bright orange-red jumping spider, one of the most colorful salticids in LA County. Males with vivid red-orange abdomen and iridescent green chelicerae used in elaborate courtship displays. Chaparral and sage scrub. Hunts by stalking and pouncing.
hp: Sage; spider predator. Chaparral. Associated with Black Sage.

desc: Silver longjawed orbweaver — elongated spider with huge, interlocking chelicerae. Builds horizontal orb webs near water. Males and females lock chelicerae face-to-face during mating — preventing the female from eating the male. Stretches along stems when resting, body parallel to twig.

fm.Body: Elongated spider with huge chelicerae

hp: Silver longjawed orbweaver — horizontal orb web near water. Males lock chelicerae during mating. Preys on midges and aquatic insects. Riparian specialist.

desc: Small house spider with distinctive zigzag triangular marks on abdomen. Extremely common indoors. Preys on other spiders including brown widows. Beneficial.
hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. Spider associate.

desc: Spined micrathena — bizarre-looking spider with sharp spines projecting from a triangular abdomen. Small orb web in woodland understory. Despite fierce appearance, completely harmless.

fm.Body: Bizarre spider with abdominal spines

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. Important spider predator in food web. Associated with Pine.

desc: Western lynx spider — green spider hunting actively on vegetation without building a web. Excellent vision from large posterior median eyes. Ambushes prey with lightning-fast strikes. Beneficial garden spider. Carries egg sac attached to spinnerets. On flowers and foliage.

fm.Body: Green spider that hunts on vegetation

hp: Active hunter — no web. Ambushes prey on flowers. In buildings. In gardens.

desc: Golden-haired tarantula of desert margins. Males wander widely in fall seeking mates, often crossing roads. At edge of range in LA County. Docile.
hp: Burrows in hillside soil. Preys on insects, other spiders. Males wander in fall seeking females. Long-lived (females 20+ years).

desc: Desert grass spider — builds sheet webs with funnel retreat in grass and low vegetation. Extremely fast — among the fastest spiders. Venom studied extensively — contains agatoxins used in neuroscience research. Not medically significant to humans. Common in grassland and gardens.

fm.Web: Funnel sheet web in grass, shrubs
fm.Behavior: Extremely fast — venom studied in neuroscience

hp: Desert grass spider — sheet web with funnel retreat. Preys on crawling/flying insects. Agatoxin venom studied in neuroscience (Adams 2004, Toxicon).

desc: Remarkable slow-moving spider that captures prey by spitting zigzag streams of sticky venomous silk from chelicerae at up to 28 m/s. Six eyes (most spiders have eight). Domed carapace houses enlarged venom/silk glands. Nocturnal. In buildings. Harmless to humans.
hp: Spitting spider — captures prey by spitting zigzag streams of venomous glue at 28 m/s (Suter & Stratton 2009, J Arachnol). Six-eyed. In buildings.

desc: Large (up to 80mm) whip scorpion that sprays concentrated acetic acid (vinegar) from base of tail when threatened. Nocturnal desert predator of insects and small vertebrates. Massive chelicerae. Harmless to humans despite terrifying appearance. Long-lived (7+ years).

fm.Venom: None — whip scorpion, not true scorpion

hp: Giant vinegaroon — sprays 84% acetic acid in defense (Eisner et al.

desc: The largest jumping spider in North America (body to 22mm in females). Black with white spots and brilliant iridescent green or blue chelicerae. Complex courtship involving leg-waving, vibration, and color display. Popular in the pet spider community.

fm.Color: Black with white spots; iridescent green chelicerae

hp: Largest jumping spider in NA (females to 22mm; Edwards 2004). Visual hunter with iridescent chelicerae. Pet trade popular.

desc: Variable-color orbweaver appearing yellow, orange, or richly marbled. Large round abdomen. Builds strong orb webs in fall. Multiple color morphs can occur in the same population. The 'pumpkin spider' — orange morphs resemble tiny pumpkins.

fm.Body: Variable color orbweaver — yellow, orange, or marbled

hp: Preys on flying insects.

desc: Southern house spider — large dark spider in old buildings. ♂ slender with long palps; ♀ stocky. Males often mistaken for brown recluse. Builds messy webs in crevices. Harmless.

fm.Color: ♀ dark brown stocky; ♂ tan slender with long palps

hp: In dark undisturbed spaces. Preys on small insects. Necrotic venom potential. Not established outdoors in LA. Spider associate.

desc: Long-legged pholcid in garages, crawl spaces, and cellars. Native California species (unlike the more common European P. phalangioides). Distinguished by subtly different male palp structure. Hangs inverted in loose irregular web.

fm.Web: Irregular web in cellars, dark spaces
fm.Behavior: Long-legged cellar spider

hp: In garages and cellars. Hangs inverted in irregular web. Native CA species. Preys on small insects.

desc: Zebra spider — tiny (5-7mm) black-and-white striped jumper commonly seen on sun-warmed walls, windows, and fences. European introduction, now cosmopolitan. Hunts flies and other small insects. Distinctive slow stalking approach followed by explosive pounce from up to 14x body length.

fm.Behavior: Stalks flies on vertical surfaces

hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. Spider associate.

desc: Jewel orbweaver — large orbweaver with distinctive horn-like shoulder bumps on abdomen giving a cat-faced appearance ('cat-faced spider'). Builds large webs on porches, eaves, and gardens in fall. Variable brown-tan. Beneficial — captures many pest insects.

fm.Color: Brown/gray with horn-like bumps on abdomen
fm.Web: Large orb web on porches, eaves

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In gardens. Preys on insects.

desc: Brilliant jumping spider — striking salticid with orange-red abdomen and black cephalothorax. Complex, multimodal courtship dance involving visual displays and vibratory signals transmitted through substrate. In meadows, grassland, and garden edges. Commonly studied species.
hp: Brilliant jumping spider — visual hunter with multimodal courtship (Clark & Uetz 1990, Anim Behav). Preys on flies and small insects on meadow vegetation.

desc: One of the most remarkable spiders in NA: males exist in two completely different forms. 'Tufted' morph is black with distinctive head tufts; 'striped' morph is gray with pale bands. Both morphs use different courtship displays. A textbook case of male polymorphism.

fm.Body: Two distinct male forms — tufted (black) and gray

hp: Two completely different male morphs with different courtship displays (Clark & Uetz 1993, Anim Behav). Textbook male polymorphism.

desc: Magnolia green jumper — translucent green jumping spider with enormous forward-facing eyes. Almost invisible on leaves. One of the few bright green jumping spiders in NA. Hunts by stealth on leaf surfaces. Retinal movement visible through translucent carapace. Long-legged for a salticid.

fm.Body: Translucent green jumping spider on leaves

hp: Preys on small flies and leafhoppers.

desc: Bronze jumper — small bronze-brown jumping spider with white lateral bands. Common on tree trunks, fence posts, and building walls. Males perform elaborate courtship: raising front legs and vibrating body. Named 'militaris' for regimented leg-waving display.

fm.Body: Small bronze-brown jumping spider on tree trunks and fences

hp: Indoor spider — beneficial pest control.

desc: Large orbweaver with round spotted abdomen. Variable — white, tan, orange, or pink morphs. Named for clover-leaf (trifolium) pattern of pale spots. Builds large webs in fall. Females guard egg sac through winter, dying after first frost.

fm.Body: Large orbweaver with round spotted abdomen

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. Important spider predator in food web. On clover.

desc: Starbellied orbweaver — distinctive small orbweaver with unique star-shaped spiny abdominal projections (no other LA orbweaver has this form). Nocturnal web-builder in shrubs and tall grass. Removes web at dawn. Brown and cream coloring provides excellent bark camouflage.

fm.Body: Small orbweaver with unique star-shaped spiny abdomen

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. Important spider predator in food web. Nocturnal. Spiny/armed.

desc: Pale yellow-green spider building silk retreat sacs in folded leaves and ceiling corners. Occasional biter — mild but annoying venom causing local swelling. Nocturnal hunter. Introduced from Mediterranean. Common indoors, especially in fall.
hp: Preys on small insects.

desc: California thin-legged wolf spider — small dark wolf spider extremely abundant on ground in diverse habitats from beach to mountain. Females carry egg sac attached to spinnerets, and carry spiderlings on abdomen after hatching. Active day and night. Important ground-level predator.

fm.Behavior: ♀ carries egg sac on spinnerets

hp: Ground-running wolf spider — nocturnal hunter. Beach/coastal habitat.

desc: Common harvestman — the classic 'daddy longlegs.' Not a spider: single-segmented body (no waist), no venom, no silk. Omnivorous scavenger. If leg is grasped by predator, it autotomizes (drops off) and keeps twitching — distracting predator. European introduction, now cosmopolitan.
hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. Predatory.

desc: Large tan solifuge (camel spider/wind scorpion) of desert margins. Enormous chelicerae — the most powerful jaws relative to body size of any arachnid. Nocturnal predator. Not venomous but can deliver a painful mechanical bite. Extremely fast runner.

fm.Habitat: Large tan solifuge of desert margins

hp: California sun spider (solifuge) — not a true spider. Enormous chelicerae crush prey mechanically. No venom. Desert nocturnal predator. Extremely fast.

desc: Book scorpion — tiny pseudoscorpion (3-4mm) with scorpion-like pincers but no tail or stinger. Beneficial household predator of booklice, dust mites, and carpet beetle larvae. In old books, behind wallpaper, in leaf litter. Rides on larger insects (phoresy) for dispersal.

fm.Behavior: Beneficial — eats booklice, dust mites

hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Beetle associate. In leaf litter.

desc: Rabbit hutch spider — small dark cobweb spider common in buildings. European introduction. Harmless relative of black widows — often found in same structures. Two faint pale dots on abdomen. Beneficial — catches flies and other household insects in tangled webs.
hp: Builds irregular cobwebs in dark sheltered spaces — garages, woodpiles, meter boxes. Spider associate.

desc: Missing-sector orbweaver — small orbweaver building distinctive incomplete web with an open sector (pie-slice gap) connected by a signal thread to the retreat. On buildings and structures. European introduction. The missing web sector is unique and diagnostic.

fm.Web: Incomplete orb with open sector + signal thread
fm.Behavior: Signal line leads to retreat — detects prey vibrations

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In buildings. Araneidae.

desc: Western feather-legged orbweaver — tiny cribellate orbweaver. One of only two spider families worldwide completely lacking venom glands (Uloboridae). Captures prey by wrapping in bands of hackled silk alone. Builds horizontal orb web with stabilimentum. Harmless.

fm.Behavior: No venom glands — wraps prey in silk only

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. Specialist spider predator.

desc: Dark ground spider of sac spider family. Found under rocks and debris in coastal sage scrub. Native to Baja and SoCal.

fm.Habitat: Under rocks, debris in coastal sage scrub

hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with Black Sage. Coastal habitat.

desc: Large, striking salticid with metallic green body and orange-bronze accents. One of the most impressive jumping spiders in LA County. On shrubs and garden vegetation. Males with elaborate courtship involving leg-waving and iridescent cheliceral display.

fm.Body: Bright metallic green jumping spider with orange markings

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Crowned jumping spider — small pale salticid with distinctive white chelicerae and crown-like thoracic marking. Common on bushes and flowers. Males with enlarged chelicerae used in face-to-face combat. Active diurnal hunter. On garden shrubs throughout LA.

fm.Body: Small pale jumping spider with white chelicerae

hp: Active hunter — no web. Ambushes prey on flowers. In gardens.

desc: Elegant jumping spider — iridescent green-bronze salticid with ant-mimicking elongated body shape. Walks with halting ant-like gait. Found on low vegetation. Males with iridescent scales that flash during courtship. An accomplished ant mimic that also preys on ants.

fm.Body: Iridescent green-bronze jumping spider

hp: Visual hunter with elaborate courtship display. Active diurnal predator on vegetation. Associated with Ash.

desc: Harmful ant-mimic jumper — elongated jumping spider that mimics ants in body shape and movement. Lives near ant trails. Despite common name, actually preys on ants. Fascinating Batesian mimicry.

fm.Body: Elongate ant-mimicking jumping spider

hp: Ant-mimicking jumping spider — walks like an ant, waves front legs like antennae. Preys on ants and small arthropods. Batesian mimicry.

desc: Mountain ant-mimic ground spider — black gnaphosid with white abdominal spots convincingly mimicking velvet ants (Dasymutilla). Nocturnal hunter under debris. The mimicry may deter predators who associate the pattern with the velvet ant's notorious sting. Mountain and foothill chaparral.

fm.Body: Black ground spider with white spots mimicking velvet ants

hp: Mimics carpenter ants.

desc: Mouse spider — velvety gray-brown nocturnal hunter resembling a small mouse in texture. Introduced from Europe, now common in houses across SoCal. Builds no web — runs down prey on walls and floors at night. Harmless to humans. Named for 19th-century English arachnologist John Blackwall.
hp: Active hunter — no web. In buildings. Specialist spider predator.

desc: Bowl-and-doily spider — tiny linyphiid building remarkable two-part web: an inverted bowl-shaped dome above a flat doily-like sheet. Insects hit bowl, fall onto doily, spider bites from below. In shrubs and garden hedges. Incredibly common but tiny and overlooked.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. In buildings. In gardens. Preys on insects.

desc: Small sheet-web spider building an inverted dome in shaded understory. Spider hangs beneath dome, biting insects that fall onto the sheet from above. In gardens, hedges, and woodland. Important prey item for forest birds. Widely distributed.

fm.Body: Small dome-web spider in shaded understory

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. In buildings. In gardens. Preys on insects.

desc: Small orbweaver with distinctive lattice-like or cross-shaped abdominal pattern in brown and cream. Nocturnal web-builder on fences, porches, and vegetation. Takes down web at dawn. Common fall species. Named for entomologist Thaddeus Harris.

fm.Body: Small orbweaver with distinctive lattice-like abdominal pattern

hp: Nocturnal predator. Araneidae.

desc: Large (4mm), strikingly velvety bright red mite emerging en masse from soil after rains. Adults are predators of insect eggs. Larvae are parasitic on insects. Beneficial garden inhabitant. The brilliant red color may be aposematic (warning coloration).
hp: Predatory. Gardens and urban areas. Warning-colored (aposematic).

desc: Tiny red-brown mite invading homes in enormous numbers in spring and fall. Feeds on clover, grass, and ivy outdoors. Leaves red stains when crushed (hemolymph, not blood). Front legs extremely long — held forward like antennae. Parthenogenetic — all females.
hp: On clover (Trifolium) and related legumes (Fabaceae). Tetranychidae.

desc: Brown bark-matching thomisid with remarkable camouflage on tree trunks, especially oaks. Ambush predator — sits motionless for hours until prey walks within reach. Cannot change color (unlike flower crab spiders). Flattened body profile matches bark contour.

fm.Body: Brown bark-matching crab spider

hp: Ambush predator on flowers or bark — grabs visiting insects with raptorial forelegs.

desc: California crab spider — dark brown ground-dwelling crab spider. Ambushes prey in leaf litter and on low vegetation. Larger and darker than flower-dwelling Misumena. Front two leg pairs enlarged for grasping. Common in natural areas — chaparral, oak woodland.

fm.Body: Dark brown crab spider active on ground and leaf litter

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Chaparral. Ambushes prey in leaf litter and on low.

desc: Common house spider — the most common cobweb spider in homes worldwide. Brown with mottled pattern. Beneficial — catches flies, mosquitoes, and other pest insects in tangled three-dimensional webs. Multiple egg sacs per female. Harmless. Also called Achaearanea tepidariorum.
hp: Most common indoor spider globally (Levi 1967). Irregular cobweb in corners and window frames. Preys on flies, ants, household insects.

desc: California trapdoor spider relative. Builds silk-lined burrow without door. Large, dark. Endemic to California.

fm.Behavior: Long-lived mygalomorph; does not build trapdoor

hp: Foothill soils.

desc: River's trapdoor spider — primitive mygalomorph building a turret-shaped silk collar around its burrow entrance (not a hinged trapdoor). Long-lived — females may survive 10+ years. California near-endemic. An ancient lineage — mygalomorphs preceded modern spiders by millions of years.

fm.Behavior: Primitive mygalomorph — builds turret not trapdoor

hp: Burrowing ambush predator. Spider specialist. Spider associate.

desc: Corner funnel weaver — builds sheet web with funnel retreat in shrubs and building corners. Extremely fast — darts from funnel to grab prey contacting web surface. Distinguished from Agelenopsis by habitat preference for shrubs and structures. Common garden spider.

fm.Body: Builds sheet web with funnel retreat in shrubs

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. In buildings. In gardens. Specialist spider predator.

desc: Western ant-mimic sac spider — dark spider convincingly mimicking carpenter ants in shape and movement. Waves front legs like antennae. Walks with halting, ant-like gait. Ground-dwelling predator that may prey on the ants it resembles. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral.

fm.Body: Dark spider mimicking carpenter ants

hp: Ant mimic — Batesian mimicry in body shape and gait. Ground predator.

desc: Severe hackledmesh weaver — dark spider building distinctive bluish-white hackled (fuzzy) cribellate webs under bark and in rock crevices. The hackled silk is produced by a cribellum organ that combs individual fibrils into woolly capture threads. Montane and foothill habitats.

fm.Body: Dark spider building cribellate webs under bark and in crevices

hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. Densely hairy.

desc: Western pirate spider — specialist spider predator that invades other spiders' webs using aggressive mimicry. Plucks silk threads to mimic trapped prey, luring the web's owner to its death. Armed with spider-specific venom causing instant paralysis on leg bite. Targets theridiids, dictynids, and araneids. The only mimetid studied ecologically in California (Kloock 2012, CSU Bakersfield).

fm.Body: Enters other spiders webs, plucks silk to lure prey

hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. Mimicry specialist.

desc: California false recluse — medium brown spider endemic to California. Frequently confused with the medically significant brown recluse (Loxosceles reclusa) due to similar coloring, but completely harmless. Key distinction: eight eyes arranged in two rows (recluse has six in three pairs). No necrotic venom.

fm.Body: Medium brown spider endemic to CA

hp: In dark undisturbed spaces.

desc: Flat, fast spider hunting on ground and bark surfaces. Long first two leg pairs held laterally like true crab spiders. Pursues prey rather than ambushing. Common in natural areas on soil and under bark. Excellent camouflage on bark substrate.

fm.Body: Flat fast spider hunting on ground and bark

hp: Active hunter — no web. On tree bark. Specialist spider predator. Cryptic camouflage.

desc: Long-palped ant-mimic — elongated dark spider with remarkably convincing ant mimicry. Waves front legs like antennae, walks with jerky ant-like gait. Long male pedipalps diagnostic. Ground-dwelling in leaf litter. Predatory — hunts small arthropods including ants.

fm.Body: Elongate dark spider mimicking large black ants

hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Ant associate. Mimicry specialist.

desc: Tiny linyphiid building dome-shaped sheet web in forest understory. Males fight on female's web — winner mates. Important prey for forest birds. The inverted dome catches insects falling from canopy. Foothill and mountain oak woodland.

fm.Behavior: Males fight on female webs

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Montane. Important prey for forest birds.

desc: Dark ground spider with distinctive white dorsal stripe resembling a clerical collar (parson's cravat). Fast nocturnal hunter under debris and in homes. Can bite if pressed against skin — mild, brief pain. Common in houses and garages.

fm.Body: Dark ground spider with white dorsal stripe (parson's cravat)

hp: Active hunter — no web. Under debris on ground. In buildings.

desc: Twin-flagged jumping spider — tiny dark salticid with two white spots on pedipalps waved like semaphore flags during elaborate courtship display. Male waves palps rhythmically. Found on ground and low vegetation. The flagging display is unique among NA jumping spiders.

fm.Body: Tiny dark jumping spider with two white spots on pedipalps waved like flags during courtship

hp: Visual hunter — stalks and pounces.

desc: Andrews's orbweaver — small orbweaver of coastal scrub and garden hedges. Variable brown-gray coloring with folium pattern on abdomen. Nocturnal web-builder — takes down web at dawn. Named for early California spider collector. Retreats to rolled leaf during day.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In gardens. Coastal habitats.

desc: Large orbweaver of warm habitats. Hides in curled leaf during day, emerging at dusk to build large orb web. Takes web down before dawn. More common in southern and coastal LA where temperatures stay warmer. Variable brown-gray coloring.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. Coastal habitats.

desc: Large wolf spider superbly camouflaged against sand. Body coloring precisely matches local beach substrate. Nocturnal hunter of beach invertebrates including amphipods and isopods. Digs shallow burrows in sand above high tide line. One of the few spiders specialized for open sandy beach habitat.
hp: Ground-running nocturnal hunter.

desc: Three-pronged lynx spider — pale green lynx spider hunting on shrubs and native vegetation. Named for three-pronged tibial apophyses in males. Ambushes prey with excellent binocular vision and rapid strikes. On sage, buckwheat, and other chaparral shrubs.
hp: Buckwheat; sage. Chaparral. Associated with California Buckwheat and Black Sage.

desc: Tiny spider that decorates its web with a vertical line of debris and dead prey — 'trash line.' Spider hides in the line, perfectly camouflaged.

fm.Color: Brown; humped abdomen; sits in debris line

hp: Specialist spider predator. Cryptic camouflage. Associated with Ash.

desc: Slender spider on vegetation. Active hunter — pounces on prey from leaves. Prominent leg spines. Important predator of crop pests.

fm.Color: Pale with dark stripes; prominent leg spines

hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. On Pinus (pine).

desc: Small dark wolf spider extremely common in gardens. Runs on ground hunting prey. Mothers carry egg sacs, then spiderlings ride on back.
hp: Active hunter — no web. In gardens. Specialist spider predator.

desc: Harvestman with spiny body. Not a spider — single body section, no silk, no venom. Common under rocks and in leaf litter in mountains.

fm.Color: Brown; spiny body; very long legs

hp: Omnivorous scavenger — dead insects, plant material, fungi. Not a spider (no venom, no silk). Autotomizes legs as defense. Montane. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org.

desc: Tiny spider building distinctive bowl-shaped web above a flat doily-shaped sheet. Very common in shrubs. Web architecture is diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark with pale marks; distinctive bowl+doily web

hp: Bowl-and-doily spider — tiny sheet-web builder in shrubs. Preys on gnats and small flies. Important prey for insectivorous birds and wasps.

desc: Large spider resembling wolf spider. Female carries egg sac in chelicerae (not on spinnerets). Builds nursery web tent for spiderlings.
hp: Hunts without web.

desc: Large flat fast spider. Not native — pantropical. Occasionally found in LA buildings. Harmless. Feared due to size but beneficial predator.
hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. Flattened body.

desc: Medium brown spider recently established in California. Originally Mediterranean. Builds sheet webs. Can bite — mildly painful.

fm.Color: Brown with distinctive fiddle-like dorsal mark

hp: Mediterranean false wolf spider — recently established in CA. Nocturnal hunter in buildings. Sheet webs. Preys on household insects.

desc: Bizarre spider that holds a small web between front legs and throws it over prey like a net. Enormous eyes for night hunting. Incredible behavior.

fm.Behavior: Holds expandable web between legs — throws it at prey

hp: Active hunter — no web. Specialist spider predator.

desc: Fast-moving solifuge (wind scorpion/camel spider). Not a spider — separate arachnid order. No venom. Enormous chelicerae. Nocturnal desert predator.

fm.Note: Not a spider — no venom, no silk

hp: Solifuge — not a true spider (separate arachnid order Solifugae). No venom — purely mechanical predator. Desert nocturnal hunter.

desc: Tiny eriophyid mite causing conspicuous blister galls on mule fat (Baccharis) leaves. Galls are green-red raised bumps. Very common in riparian areas.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; galls are raised blisters on mule fat leaves

hp: Microscopic plant gall-inducer. On Baccharis (coyote brush).

desc: Large ground-running wolf spider of open habitats. Nocturnal. Eyes reflect bright green with headlamp. Females carry egg sacs and young on their backs.

fm.Behavior: Eyes glow green with headlamp at night

hp: Ground-running hunter — pursues prey rather than using a web. Carries egg sac. Spider specialist.

desc: Dark jumping spider with distinctive white lateral stripes. Very common on tree bark and fences. Hunts actively by day. Often found on building exteriors.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with white lateral stripes

hp: Ribbon jumping spider — diurnal visual hunter on tree trunks and fences. White lateral stripes. Preys on small flies and bark-dwelling insects.

desc: Medium brown scorpion common in foothill and desert habitats. Found under rocks and bark. Mildly venomous — sting comparable to bee sting. Glows under UV light.

fm.Color: Brown; fluoresces blue-green under UV light
fm.Warning: Mild sting — comparable to bee sting

hp: Bioluminescent — produces light for mate attraction or warning. Remarkable adaptation. Desert specialist.

desc: Pale fast-running spider active at night. Builds no web — hunts by running down prey on foliage. Often found on walls at night near porch lights.
hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. Spider associate.

desc: Trashline orbweaver — tiny spider decorating its web with a vertical line of debris. Spider sits motionless in the debris line, invisible to both predators and prey.

fm.Color: Brown; humped abdomen; hides in debris line

hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. Associated with Ash.

desc: Medium brown orb weaver building webs in coastal scrub and chaparral. Nocturnal — takes down web at dawn. Common but secretive.

fm.Color: Brown with subtle markings; nocturnal web builder

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In buildings. Chaparral habitat. Coastal habitats.

desc: Jewel spider — large orb weaver with gem-like tubercles on abdomen. Builds enormous webs on buildings at night. Very common in suburban LA. Harmless.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In buildings. Urban habitat.

cn: California Black Walnut Pouch Gall Mite
desc: Willow gall mite — microscopic eriophyid mite inducing conspicuous raised bead-like galls on willow leaves. Very common along riparian corridors. Galls are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; galls are raised beads on willow leaves

hp: Gall-inducer on willows (salix). Riparian habitat.

desc: Sagebrush gall mite — microscopic eriophyid mite causing enlarged distorted buds on sagebrush (Artemisia). Common in coastal sage scrub. Galls are swollen fuzzy buds.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; galls are swollen buds on sagebrush

hp: Gall-inducer on host plant. Associated with California Sagebrush. Coastal habitat.

desc: Medium brown scorpion of LA County foothills and canyons. Found under rocks and bark. Fluorescent blue-green under UV light. Mild sting.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. UV fluorescent — glows under blacklight.

desc: Builds huge orb webs at night, takes them down by dawn. Hides in curled leaf during day. Distinctly spotted abdomen. Common in gardens.

fm.Color: Brown with pale spots; nocturnal web builder

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In gardens. In gardens and urban areas. Spotted pattern.

desc: Gertsch's sawfinger scorpion — small scorpion with distinctively serrated pedipalp fingers. Desert and foothill habitats. Nocturnal. Found under rocks. Mild sting.
hp: Nocturnal ambush predator. Detects prey by ground vibrations. UV-fluorescent. Desert specialist.

desc: Tiny spider building small flat star-shaped webs on walls and ceilings. Extremely common on building exteriors worldwide. Often near light fixtures where it catches tiny insects.

fm.Color: Pale gray; flat star-shaped web on walls

hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. Flattened body.

desc: Dark robust scorpion. Found in rocky foothill habitats. Nocturnal. One of the larger scorpions in LA County mountains.
hp: Nocturnal ambush predator. Detects prey by ground vibrations. UV-fluorescent. Superstitioniidae.

desc: Tiny predatory arachnid resembling a scorpion without a tail. Lives in leaf litter. Grasps prey with pincers. Beneficial predator of mites and springtails.

fm.Color: Red-brown; scorpion-like pincers but no tail

hp: Nocturnal ambush predator. Detects prey by ground vibrations. UV-fluorescent. Predatory.

desc: Small fast-running spider recently established in SoCal. Pantropical introduction. Found under debris in gardens. Builds no web.
hp: Active hunter — no web. Under debris on ground. In gardens.

desc: Small-medium scorpion found in foothill canyons of the San Gabriel and Santa Monica Mountains. Nocturnal. Under rocks. Glows under UV.
hp: Bioluminescent — produces light for mate attraction or warning. Remarkable adaptation. Canyon habitat.

desc: Medium-sized tick; major vector of Rocky Mountain spotted fever. Found in grassy and shrubby areas. Attaches to dogs, humans, and wildlife.

fm.Size: ⅛ in unfed; ½ in engorged
fm.Warning: Disease vector — check for ticks after hiking

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Ixodidae.

desc: Pacific Coast tick — common tick of chaparral and grassland. Primary vector of 364D rickettsiosis in California. Attaches to deer, dogs, and humans.

fm.Color: Brown with ornate pale dorsal markings

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. In chaparral habitat.

desc: One of the tiniest jumping spiders in LA County at barely 2mm. Dark brown with pale markings. Despite its microscopic size, an active visual predator stalking prey on low vegetation and leaf litter. Part of the primarily Neotropical Mexigonus genus.
hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Spider specialist. In leaf litter.

desc: California trapdoor spider — large primitive spider building silk-lined burrows with trapdoor. Massive chelicerae. Nocturnal. Foothill and mountain habitats. California endemic.
hp: Burrowing ambush predator. Spider specialist. Nocturnal.

desc: Tiny jumping spider — males perform elaborate courtship dances with colorful displays. Complex choreography studied by behavioral biologists.

fm.Color: ♂ colorful face+legs for courtship; ♀ cryptic brown

hp: Active ground hunter. Males perform elaborate multimodal courtship dances with colorful ornaments.

desc: McCook's wolf spider — medium wolf spider with tufted front legs used in courtship signaling. Males drum and wave leg tufts to females. Common in leaf litter.

fm.Color: Brown with pale median stripe; ♂ tufted front legs

hp: Ground-running hunter — pursues prey rather than using a web. Carries egg sac.

desc: Small gray jumping spider on building walls. Males wave enlarged palps during courtship. Very common on exterior walls and fences.

fm.Color: Gray with white markings; on walls

hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Small. Spider specialist. Spider associate.

desc: Small ground spider that remarkably mimics ants in body shape and movement. Walks with front legs raised like antennae. On ground among ants.

fm.Color: Dark; ant-shaped body; mimics ant movement

hp: Mimicry — Batesian or aggressive. Spider specialist.

desc: Willow eriophyid mite — microscopic mite causing curled and distorted willow leaves. Very common on willows along streams. Galls are diagnostic.
hp: Gall-inducer on willows (salix). Eriophyidae.

desc: Long-palped lynx spider — bright green lynx spider similar to P. viridans but with longer palps. Ambush predator on flowers. Mothers guard egg sacs.

fm.Color: Bright green; spiny legs; long palps

hp: Long-palped lynx spider — ambushes pollinators on chaparral flowers. Green body camouflages among foliage. Prey for birds.

desc: Gray lynx spider — small gray lynx spider on vegetation. Active predator. Prominent leg spines. Less common than striped and green lynx spiders.
hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. On Pinus (pine).

desc: Tiny bright green orb weaver with six black spots on underside. Builds small orbs in shrubs. Very common but easily overlooked.
hp: Tiny green orb weaver on shrubs. Builds small orb web among leaves. Preys on small flying insects.

desc: Medium orb weaver with intricate abdominal pattern. Builds web at dusk, removes at dawn. Common in gardens and woodland edges.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In gardens. In gardens and urban areas. Arabesque.

desc: Dark ground spider remarkably resembling a large carpenter ant. Runs with ant-like gait. Predatory. Found among ants.
hp: Ground-hunting spider predator. Ant mimic.

desc: Dewdrop spider — tiny silver spider living as kleptoparasite in larger spiders' webs. Steals prey from host's web. Globular silver abdomen reflects light.
hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. Parasitoid.

desc: Poison oak leaf mite — microscopic eriophyid mite causing red blister galls on poison oak leaves. Very common wherever poison oak grows. Galls are raised red bumps.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; red blister galls on poison oak leaves

hp: Gall-inducer on coast live oak. On Quercus (oak).

desc: Small tan jumping spider common in chaparral vegetation. Active day-hunting predator with excellent vision. Males perform courtship displays.
hp: Mann's jumping spider — diurnal visual hunter in chaparral vegetation. Preys on small flies, leafhoppers. Common native salticid.

desc: Small dark jumper of chaparral and sage scrub. Active day hunter on vegetation. Males with iridescent scales.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. In chaparral habitat. Iridescent.

desc: Large dark trapdoor spider building silk-lined burrows with hinged door. California endemic. Nocturnal — waits with door slightly ajar to ambush prey.
hp: Burrowing ambush predator. Spider specialist. Nocturnal.

desc: Tiny spider living in splash zone of rocky shores. One of the few spiders adapted to marine intertidal habitat. Feeds on sea slaters and beach flies.

fm.Habitat: Rocky intertidal splash zone — unusual for a spider

hp: Intertidal specialist — one of few spiders living in ocean splash zone. Preys on beach invertebrates.

desc: Tiny jumping spider with elaborate male courtship. Males display colorful face and leg ornaments with complex choreography. Studied extensively.
hp: Active ground hunter. Males with colorful ornamental leg segments used in courtship displays.

desc: Medium jumper with dense body hair. Hunts actively on vegetation and bark. Common in chaparral and woodland.
hp: Hairy tufted jumping spider — diurnal visual hunter on chaparral vegetation. Dense body hair. Preys on flies and small insects.

desc: Microscopic mite inducing abnormal growth (galls) on aloe plants. Common on ornamental aloes in LA gardens. Causes warty/distorted growth.

fm.Color: Invisible; warty galls on aloe plants

hp: Microscopic plant gall-inducer.

desc: Schlingeri's trapdoor spider — large dark trapdoor spider building silk-lined burrows. California endemic. Nocturnal — ambushes prey from burrow entrance.
hp: Burrowing ambush predator. Spider specialist. Nocturnal.

desc: Trapdoor spider of foothill habitats. California endemic. Complex courtship — males drum on female's trapdoor to announce presence.
hp: Trapdoor builder in foothill soils. California endemic. Long-lived female guards burrow.

desc: Bright green crab spider on flowers and foliage. Ambush predator. Can change shade slightly to match background. Common in gardens.
hp: Ambush predator. Gardens and urban areas.

desc: Medium jumping spider with bright green chelicerae. Active day-hunter. Common on fences, walls, and vegetation. Bold.
hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Spider associate.

desc: Medium burrowing wolf spider of open sandy habitats. Nocturnal. Eyes reflect green with headlamp. Females carry egg sacs.

fm.Color: Brown; in burrows in sandy soil

hp: Ground-running hunter — pursues prey rather than using a web. Carries egg sac.

desc: Microscopic mite causing fuzzy erineum (felt-like growth) on live oak leaf undersides. Very common. Galls are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Invisible; fuzzy felt patches on oak leaf undersides

hp: Gall-inducer on coast live oak. On Quercus (oak). Densely hairy.

desc: Large burrowing wolf spider active at dawn and dusk. Sits at burrow entrance waiting for prey. Eyes glow bright green with headlamp at night.

fm.Color: Brown; large; eyes glow green at night

hp: Large burrowing wolf spider — sits at burrow entrance at dusk ambushing passing insects. Nocturnal ground predator. Female carries egg sac.

desc: Tiny jumping spider with ornate ♂ courtship display. Males wave colorful legs and palps in complex choreography. On ground in open habitats.
hp: Active visual hunter. Males with elaborate courtship involving colorful leg and palp displays.

desc: Medium orb weaver with wing-shaped abdominal projections resembling a butterfly. Unusual angular shape. Builds orb webs in shrubs.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects.

desc: Microscopic eriophyid inducing enlarged distorted buds on ragweed. Very common wherever ragweed grows. Galls are diagnostic swollen buds.

fm.Color: Invisible; swollen bud galls on ragweed

hp: Microscopic plant gall-inducer. Each species typically host-specific. Induces species-specific gall structures on host plant.

desc: Small brown-tan jumper of dry open habitats. Active day-hunter with excellent vision. On ground and low vegetation.
hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Small. Spider specialist. Salticidae.

desc: Long-legged harvestman (daddy longlegs) of oak woodland. Not a spider — no venom, no silk. Scavenges small invertebrates and plant material.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Scavenger.

desc: Microscopic mite causing raised blister galls on chamise (Adenostoma) leaves. Very common in chaparral — chamise is the dominant shrub.

fm.Color: Invisible; blister galls on chamise leaves

hp: Gall-inducer on host plant. Associated with Chamise.

desc: Hairy paradise spider — tiny furry jumping spider with complex male courtship dance involving colorful displays. On ground in chaparral. Part of the speciose Habronattus radiation.
hp: Visual hunter with elaborate courtship display. Active diurnal predator on vegetation.

desc: Medium yellowish scorpion of foothill and desert habitats. Mildly venomous — sting like a bee. Under rocks. Fluoresces under UV light.
hp: Desert scorpion — ambush predator at burrow entrance. UV fluorescent — glows under blacklight.

desc: Medium pale scorpion of desert and sandy habitats. Nocturnal. Glows blue-green under UV light. Mild sting — not medically significant.
hp: Bioluminescent — produces light for mate attraction or warning. Remarkable adaptation. Desert specialist.

desc: Brown bark-colored crab spider ambushing prey on tree trunks. Flat body pressed against bark. Common on oaks and pines.

fm.Color: Brown bark-pattern; flat against tree trunk

hp: Bark crab spider ambushing prey on oak and pine trunks. Cryptic bark-matching coloring.

desc: Small spider building irregular meshwork webs on vegetation. Common in chaparral. Part of the diverse meshweb spider family.

fm.Color: Brown; small meshwork web on plants

hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Flat-bodied salticid living on tree bark and walls. Flattened shape adapted for life in narrow crevices. Hunts by day on sun-warmed surfaces.
hp: Visual hunter — stalks and pounces on prey. Flattened body.

desc: Medium bold jumping spider. Active diurnal hunter on vegetation and fences. Excellent binocular vision. Curious and approachable.
hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Diurnal.

desc: Tiny cobweb spider common in buildings. Builds small tangle webs in corners. Variable coloring. One of many small theridiids indoors.
hp: Builds irregular tangle web. In buildings. On corn.

desc: Microscopic mite causing fuzzy felt patches (erinia) on coast live oak leaf undersides. Very common. Felt patches turn brown with age.

fm.Color: Invisible; fuzzy felt patches on oak leaves

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Densely hairy.

desc: Small salticid named for the renowned spider taxonomist Jerzy Proszynski. Active day-hunter on vegetation and buildings.
hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Small. Spider specialist. Spider associate.

desc: Medium orb-weaving spider building webs in shrubs and low vegetation. Distinctive leaf-shaped abdominal pattern.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In buildings. Spider associate.

desc: Small short-legged harvestman of moist habitats. Unlike typical daddy longlegs, has shorter legs. Under rocks and in leaf litter in canyons.

fm.Color: Brown; short legs for a harvestman

hp: Omnivorous scavenger — dead insects, plant material, fungi. Family Nemastomatidae. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org.

desc: Tiny dark jumping spider of ground-level habitats. In leaf litter and under rocks in chaparral and oak woodland. Belairbirder iNat regular. Part of the primarily Neotropical Mexigonus radiation, with two species reaching LA County.
hp: Oak; rose. Chaparral. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Microscopic mite causing blister galls on coyote brush (Baccharis) leaves. Raised bumps on leaves diagnostic. Common in sage scrub.

fm.Color: Invisible; blister galls on Baccharis leaves

hp: Gall mite on Baccharis pilularis (coyote brush) — induces blister galls on leaves. Microscopic. Native mite on native coastal sage scrub shrub.

desc: Flattened pale jumper on building walls. Originally Mediterranean, now established in SoCal. Hunts small insects on sun-warmed surfaces.
hp: Visual hunter — stalks and pounces on prey. Flattened body. Half-edged.

desc: Ground spider mimicking ants in shape and movement. Walks with front pair of legs raised like antennae. Predatory — hunts among ants.
hp: Ground-hunting spider predator. Ant mimic.

desc: Extremely tiny salticid. Among the smallest jumping spiders in LA County. On ground and leaf litter. Active hunter despite microscopic size.
hp: Among smallest jumping spiders in NA (~2mm). Active visual hunter in leaf litter. Metallic body. Easily overlooked but locally common.

desc: Schlinger's paradise spider — tiny jumping spider with elaborate ♂ courtship involving colorful displays and complex choreography. Named for Schlinger. Ground-dwelling.
hp: Active ground hunter. Males display colorful ornaments during elaborate courtship dances.

desc: Small spider that spits a mixture of silk and venom onto prey from a distance. Slow-moving nocturnal predator. Striped legs distinctive.

fm.Behavior: Spits silk+venom at prey from distance

hp: Predator of insects and other arthropods.

desc: Medium orb weaver of woodland and garden edges. Builds web at dusk. Variable patterning. Part of the common Araneus genus.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In gardens. In gardens and urban areas. Detrimental.

desc: Small dark ground spider with three pale stripes. Fast nocturnal runner under debris. Ant-mimicking body shape.

fm.Color: Dark with 3 pale stripes; ant-like shape

hp: Under debris on ground. Ant mimic — may prey on ants.

desc: Tiny introduced salticid. Flat body adapted for living under bark and in crevices. On building exteriors. Originally from East Asia.
hp: Visual ambush hunter with acute eyesight. Stalks prey on vegetation by day.

desc: Tiny linyphiid spider building horizontal sheet webs in low vegetation. Common but overlooked due to minute size. Important predator of tiny insects.

fm.Color: Dark; tiny sheet web in low vegetation

hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. Predatory.

desc: Small short-legged harvestman of moist canyon habitats. California native. Under rocks and in leaf litter. Unusual lobed body shape.
hp: Omnivorous scavenger — dead insects, plant material, fungi. Family Phalangodidae. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org.

desc: Medium dark tarantula of foothill and mountain habitats. Nocturnal. Males wander in fall seeking females. California endemic.
hp: Burrows in hillside soil. Preys on insects, other spiders. Males wander in fall seeking females. Long-lived (females 20+ years).

desc: Long-legged harvestman of scrub and woodland. Named for naturalist T.D.A. Cockerell. Scavenges on small invertebrates.
hp: Omnivorous scavenger — dead insects, plant material, fungi. Not a spider (no venom, no silk). Autotomizes legs as defense. Woodland. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org.

desc: Very elongated pale spider that stretches along grass blades and plant stems to hide. Extremely well camouflaged. Active hunter, no web.

fm.Color: Pale tan; very elongated; aligns with stems

hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. Spider associate.

desc: Tiny arachnid (schizomid) living in leaf litter and soil. Eyeless. Whip-like tail. Rarely seen — one of the most obscure LA arachnids.
hp: Nocturnal ambush predator. Detects prey by ground vibrations. UV-fluorescent. In leaf litter.

desc: Microscopic eriophyid mite inducing galls on scalebroom (Lepidospartum). Host-specific. Part of the enormous but invisible world of plant gall mites.
hp: Gall-inducer on host plant. Scale insect associate.

desc: Long-spined meshweaver — tiny spider building irregular mesh webs in leaf litter. Thread-like web strands. Microscopic — requires close examination to find.
hp: Tiny mesh-web spider in conifer branches. Builds cribellate hackled-band web among pine needles. Preys on small flying insects.

desc: Elongated spider with long chelicerae. Stretches along twigs when disturbed. Builds orb webs near water. Males interlock jaws during mating.

fm.Color: Variable tan/green; long jaws; near water

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects.

desc: Large dark mygalomorph building silk-lined burrows with trapdoor. California endemic. Mountain and foothill habitats. Nocturnal ambush predator.
hp: Predator of insects and other arthropods.

desc: Small reddish-brown ground spider. Fast nocturnal runner. Introduced from Mediterranean. Found under rocks and debris in gardens.
hp: Nocturnal predator. Garden associate.

desc: Small pale spider with dark cardiac mark. Fast nocturnal hunter in leaf litter and under rocks. Part of the diverse ground spider family Gnaphosidae.
hp: Nocturnal predator. Spider specialist. In leaf litter.

desc: Bridge spider — large grey-brown orb weaver that favors man-made structures, especially bridges, buildings, and fences near lights that attract prey. Introduced from Europe.
hp: Builds orb web in vegetation to capture flying insects. Prey for birds.

desc: Large flat salticid found under bark. Powerful jumper with excellent binocular vision. Hunts during the day. On tree trunks and fences.
hp: Visual hunter — stalks and pounces on prey. Flattened body.

desc: Small brown jumper of mountain and foothill habitats. Active day-hunter stalking prey on vegetation. Part of the diverse salticid fauna.
hp: Active hunter — no web. Specialist spider predator.

desc: Tiny metallic-bronze salticid. Active day-hunter on low vegetation. Males with iridescent chelicerae used in courtship displays.
hp: Visual hunter with elaborate courtship display. Active diurnal predator on vegetation.

desc: Brown orb weaver named for Monterey County. Builds webs in garden shrubs and trees. Variable coloring. Nocturnal.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In gardens. In gardens and urban areas. California.

desc: Medium orb weaver building webs in sheltered spots like caves, rock overhangs, and buildings. Related to Tetragnatha.

fm.Color: Brown; in sheltered webs — caves, overhangs

hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In buildings. Tetragnathidae.

desc: Small elongated gnaphosid. Nocturnal runner in leaf litter. Dark with pale markings. Ant-like movement pattern may deter predators.
hp: Ground-hunting spider predator.

desc: Slender orb weaver with elongated abdomen and fine longitudinal stripe. Builds webs in tall grass and reeds near water. Nocturnal.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. Important spider predator in food web.

desc: Small dark theridiid building irregular tangle webs. Related to true widows but much less dangerous. Under eaves and in garages.
hp: Builds irregular tangle web. In buildings. Associated with Ash.

desc: Large pale scorpion of desert habitats. Nocturnal. Largest scorpion genus in North America. UV fluorescent. Moderate sting.
hp: Nocturnal ambush predator. Detects prey by ground vibrations. UV-fluorescent. Large pale scorpion of desert habitats. Nocturnal. Largest s.

desc: Tiny spider mite causing silvering damage on avocado leaves. Builds webbing nests on leaf undersides. Important pest of California avocados.

fm.Color: Invisible; silvering damage on avocado leaves

hp: Sap-feeding pest of plants. On avocado.

desc: Tiny theridiid with spotted legs. In vegetation and on buildings. Builds small irregular webs. Common but easily overlooked.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Spider specialist.

desc: Small orb weaver with two small abdominal humps. In shrubs and low vegetation. Variable coloring. Part of the species-rich Araneus genus.
hp: Two-spined orbweaver — builds orb web in conifer branches and chaparral shrubs. Nocturnal web-builder. Preys on moths and flies.

desc: Large mygalomorph of coastal dune habitats. Builds silk-lined burrow with trapdoor. California endemic. Threatened by coastal development.
hp: Ambush predator.

desc: Devastating ectoparasite of honey bees. Feeds on bee fat bodies. Primary driver of colony collapse disorder worldwide. Vectors bee viruses. Critical agricultural threat.

fm.Warning: Primary cause of honey bee colony losses

hp: Parasitoid of solitary bee/wasp nests. Varroidae.

desc: Two-spotted spider mite — tiny red-green mite causing stippled yellowing on plants. One of the most important agricultural pests worldwide. Fine webbing diagnostic.
hp: Two-spotted spider mite — most economically important mite pest worldwide (Jeppson et al. 1975). Damages 1,100+ plant species. Produces silk webbing.

desc: Small dark theridiid. Builds irregular cobwebs under objects. Less common than S. grossa. Similar appearance to black widows but harmless.
hp: Builds irregular cobwebs in dark sheltered spaces — garages, woodpiles, meter boxes. Theridiidae.

desc: Tiny jumping spider with ornate male courtship involving colorful leg and palp displays. Ground-dwelling in open habitats.
hp: Active ground hunter. Males with ornate courtship involving colored leg and body displays.

desc: The only tick that can complete its entire life cycle indoors. Primarily parasitizes dogs. Can transmit canine ehrlichiosis and babesiosis. Infests kennels and homes.
hp: Brown dog tick — primary ectoparasite of domestic dogs. Can vector canine ehrlichiosis and babesiosis. Infests kennels and homes.

desc: Small brown orb weaver resembling European Zilla. Builds webs in garden shrubs. Nocturnal. Common but easily overlooked.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. In gardens. In gardens and urban areas. Zilla-like.

desc: Microscopic eriophyid mite causing distinctive felt-like patches (erinea) on grape leaf undersides. Upper leaf surface shows blistered bumps. Common on backyard grapevines.
hp: Microscopic plant gall-inducer. Each species typically host-specific. Induces species-specific gall structures on host plant. On grape.

desc: Red-and-white cobweb spider — small theridiid with distinctive red prosoma and white abdomen markings. In leaf litter and under debris. Less commonly seen than other theridiids.
hp: Builds irregular tangle web. In leaf litter.

desc: Tiny dictynid spider of California. Builds small mesh webs. Poorly known. Part of the cryptic ground-dwelling spider community.
hp: Cryptically camouflaged against habitat substrate. Tiny. Linsdale's.

desc: Small theridiid building tent-like web. Males also self-amputate one palp. Related to T. haemorrhoidale.
hp: Builds irregular tangle web. In buildings.

desc: Tiny blue-black mite pest of winter cereals and pasture grasses. Important agricultural pest. Feeds on grass leaves causing silvering.
hp: Grassland/rangeland herbivore. On Poaceae (grasses).

desc: Tiny jumping spider with ornate male courtship. Males wave decorated leg segments in complex dances. Studied by behavioral biologists.

fm.Color: ♂ ornate legs for courtship dance; ♀ brown

hp: Active visual hunter. Males wave decorated tarsal segments during courtship display.

desc: Marks's scorpion — medium desert scorpion fluorescing bright green-blue under UV light (all scorpions fluoresce, but this species is easily found by UV). Nocturnal ambush predator of insects. Mild sting — far less venomous than Centruroides. Sandy desert habitats.
hp: Nocturnal ambush predator. Detects prey by ground vibrations. UV-fluorescent. Desert specialist.

desc: Small uniformly brown-tan jumper on vegetation in chaparral. The unusually uniform coloring (hence 'concoloratus') distinguishes it from most salticids which have bold patterns. Active day-hunter. Males with subtle courtship display.
hp: Active hunter — no web. Chaparral habitat.

desc: Large mygalomorph of foothill habitats. Silk-lined burrow with trapdoor. California endemic. Restricted range.
hp: Silk-lined burrow with wafer trapdoor. Ambush predator. Foothill endemic. Ancient mygalomorph lineage.

desc: Tiny pale yellow harvestman of caves and rocky crevices. Cave-adapted with reduced pigment. California endemic.
hp: Omnivorous scavenger — dead insects, plant material, fungi. Not a spider (no venom, no silk). Autotomizes legs as defense. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org.

desc: Medium-large salticid with eight pale spots on black abdomen. Desert and sage scrub specialist. Active diurnal hunter. Males with iridescent green chelicerae used in courtship. One of the larger Phidippus in western desert habitats.

fm.Color: Dark with eight pale spots; desert

hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with Black Sage. Diurnal/day-active.

desc: Tiny red predatory mite of coastal habitats. On rocks and beach debris. Predatory on other mites and small arthropods.
hp: Predatory — hunts arthropod prey. Coastal habitat.

desc: Small angular crab spider resembling a twig bump. Remarkable camouflage. Sits motionless on branches waiting for prey.
hp: Specialist spider predator. Cryptic camouflage.

desc: Small orb weaver with angular heptagonal (7-sided) abdomen shape. Distinctive silhouette. In shrubs.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Araneidae.

desc: Small theridiid with cockroach-brown coloring. In buildings and under debris. Builds irregular cobwebs.
hp: Builds irregular tangle web. In buildings. Preys on cockroach.

desc: Small brown jumper on native chaparral shrubs. Active day-hunter. Part of the diverse but difficult-to-identify Metaphidippus complex.
hp: Active hunter — no web. Chaparral habitat. Preys on aphids.

desc: Small tan-brown salticid of chaparral vegetation. Active day-hunter with excellent binocular vision. Males with ornate courtship displays involving leg-waving.
hp: Active hunter — no web. Chaparral habitat.

desc: Medium spider of rocky habitats and caves. Member of the family Plectreuridae — a California near-endemic family found almost nowhere else on Earth. In rock crevices and caves in foothill habitats. Important for biogeography — ancient relict lineage.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Spider associate.

desc: Small fast-running spider. Nocturnal. Under debris. Part of the poorly known Trachelidae.
hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. Nocturnal.

desc: Large dark spider building messy webs in crevices and holes in buildings. Related to southern house spider. Harmless.

fm.Color: Dark; messy web in crevices; harmless

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Spider specialist.

desc: Small cosmopolitan jumping spider found in houses worldwide. Bold day-hunter on walls. Introduced from Old World tropics.

fm.Color: Dark with white spots; on indoor walls

hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Introduced species.

desc: Enigma eriophyid mite — microscopic mite (<0.2mm) inducing galls on native plants. Part of the hyperdiverse Eriophyidae — thousands of species, most undescribed, each typically host-specific. Invisible without magnification. The 'enigma' name reflects difficult taxonomy.
hp: Microscopic plant gall-inducer. Family Eriophyidae. Part of the hyperdiverse Eriophyidae — thousands of.

desc: Medium brown spider of foothill habitats. Fast runner. Part of the poorly known Tengellidae. California native.
hp: Fast-running ground hunter in foothill habitats. Part of the poorly known Tengellidae.

desc: Icenogle's trapdoor spider — trapdoor spider of foothill habitats. California endemic. Named for arachnologist Icenogle.
hp: Trapdoor burrow in foothill soils. California endemic. Named for arachnologist who discovered many CA trapdoor spiders.

desc: Green crab spider NAMED FOR LEONARDO DICAPRIO in recognition of his environmental advocacy. On flowers and foliage. California.

fm.Note: Named for Leonardo DiCaprio — environmental honor

hp: On flowers. Part of flower-visiting community. Spider associate.

desc: Large dark mygalomorph building silk-lined burrows. California endemic. Nocturnal ambush predator.
hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Spider specialist. Nocturnal.

desc: Small flower crab spider named for researcher Aiko. Ambush predator on flower heads — can slowly change color over days to match flower (white, yellow, or pink). Grabs pollinating insects with enlarged forelegs. On native and garden flowers.
hp: Flower crab spider — changes color (white/yellow/pink) over days to match bloom. Ambushes bees, flies, butterflies visiting flowers.

desc: Microscopic eriophyid mite inducing galls on native plants. Part of the vast community of invisible plant-gall-forming mites that shape leaf and bud development.
hp: Microscopic plant gall-inducer. Family Eriophyidae. Neo-Beevor's.

desc: Beautiful triangulate cobweaver — small theridiid with distinctive triangular abdomen. Builds irregular tangle web in vegetation and under eaves. Named for pioneer American arachnologist J.H. Emerton. In low vegetation and building crevices. Beneficial garden predator.
hp: Predatory — hunts arthropod prey. Beneficial in gardens. In gardens and urban areas.
