desc: Tiny arachnid (schizomid) in soil. Eyeless. Whip-like tail. Related to vinegaroons but microscopic. California endemic.

fm.Note: Schizomid — separate arachnid order; CA endemic

hp: Desert soil predator. Sprays acetic acid defense. Preys on insects, millipedes, small vertebrates. Under rocks.

desc: Medium wolf spider of wetland margins. Runs on water surface to catch prey. Can dive underwater to escape predators.
hp: Ground-running hunter — pursues prey rather than using a web. Carries egg sac. Spider specialist.

desc: Tiny colorful jumper on plants. Active day-hunter. Part of diverse western salticid fauna.
hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Papenhoe's.

desc: Medium brown spider building sheet webs with funnel retreat in buildings, barns, and cellars. Harmless. One of the most common indoor spiders worldwide.

fm.Color: Brown with chevron marks; funnel web indoors

hp: Barn funnel weaver — among most common house spiders worldwide. Sheet web with funnel retreat. Preys on household insects.

desc: Medium bold jumper. Like other Phidippus, curious and willing to investigate humans. Green chelicerae.
hp: Active diurnal hunter on vegetation. Curious and bold — investigates approaching objects. On flowers and shrubs.

desc: Gertsch's triangle spider — tiny spider building triangular web (only 4 radii). Holds web taut, vibrates it when prey hits. Unique web architecture.

fm.Note: Triangular web — vibrates to snare prey

hp: Predator of insects and small arthropods. Part of garden and habitat food web. Spider associate.

desc: Tiny linyphiid spider. Ballooning dispersal — uses silk threads to fly on wind. Very common but microscopic.
hp: Tiny sheet web in low vegetation. Ballooning dispersal — uses silk threads to fly on wind currents.

desc: Tiny jumper with crisp/curly markings. On vegetation. Active day-hunter. Named for the genus's connection to old English.
hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Salticidae.

desc: Tiny bright red predatory mite. Extremely fast runner for its size. Important predator of pest mites and small insects on plants. Beneficial.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Very small species.

desc: Microscopic parasitic mite living in ear canals of dogs and cats. Causes intense itching and secondary infections. Common veterinary pest.
hp: Ectoparasitic mite — feeds on host body fluids or skin.

desc: Microscopic burrowing mite causing scabies in humans. Tunnels under skin causing intense itching. Highly contagious. Important medical pest.
hp: Burrowing ectoparasite under human skin. Causes scabies. Microscopic. Veterinary and public health concern.

desc: Tiny spider mite causing brown stippling on avocado leaves. Important pest of California avocados. Webbing on leaf undersides.
hp: Sap-feeding pest of plants. On avocado.

desc: Tiny theridiid. Males amputate one palp before mating — only spider known to do this. Bizarre reproductive biology.

fm.Note: Males amputate one palp before mating — unique

hp: Male amputates one palp before mating — unique self-mutilation (Ramos et al. 2004, Nature). Cobweb in dry habitats. Tiny male, large female.

desc: Small green-brown orb weaver frequently found on rose bushes and other garden shrubs. Builds vertical orb web at night, retreats to vegetation by day. Variable brown-green coloring. Common in LA gardens but small and easily overlooked.
hp: Builds orb web to capture flying insects. Important spider predator in food web. Associated with Rose.

desc: Tiny jumping spider. Males with colorful courtship ornaments. Part of the most intensively studied courtship behavior genus.
hp: Active ground hunter. Males with colorful ornamental features for courtship display.

desc: Large soft tick found in deer bedding areas in mountains. Extremely painful bite — 'pajahuello' is Spanish for pest. Can cause allergic reactions.

fm.Warning: Extremely painful bite — allergic reactions possible

hp: Feeds on deer and other mammals.

desc: Tiny pale dictynid spider building small irregular mesh webs on vegetation and building walls. Hackled silk produced by cribellum. Colonial — multiple webs often clustered together. Very common on stucco walls and window frames throughout LA.
hp: Indoor spider — beneficial pest control.

desc: Microscopic mite causing abnormal flower galls on ash trees. Flowers become swollen and persist as brown clusters.
hp: Microscopic plant gall-inducer. Associated with Ash.

desc: Small nocturnal hunting spider building silk retreats ('sacs') on foliage. Active night hunter.

fm.Color: Pale brown; silk retreat on leaves

hp: Nocturnal predator. Spider specialist. Spider associate.

desc: Microscopic mite infesting citrus buds causing distorted fruit ('lemon drop' fruit). Important citrus pest. Damages developing buds.
hp: Citrus bud mite — microscopic eriophyid distorting citrus buds and fruit. Major pest of lemons (Grafton-Cardwell et al. 2003).

desc: Medium spider of rock crevices and caves. Part of the nearly California-endemic family Plectreuridae.
hp: In rock crevices and caves. Plectreuridae — California near-endemic family. Ancient relict.

desc: Lantana gall mite — microscopic mite causing flower galls on lantana. Introduced for biocontrol of invasive lantana.
hp: Biological control agent — predator of mites.

desc: Small striped salticid on vegetation. Active day-hunter with acute vision. Part of the well-studied Pelegrina genus. On shrubs in gardens and chaparral.
hp: Visual hunter — stalks and pounces.

desc: Tiny jumping spider from Catalina Island area. Among the smallest jumping spiders. Hunts in leaf litter.
hp: Among smallest jumping spiders — body ~2mm. Visual hunter in leaf litter. LA County near-endemic (Griswold 1987). Dark iridescent.

desc: Medium wolf spider living in permanent silk-lined burrow. Sits at entrance waiting for prey. Desert habitats.

fm.Color: Brown; in permanent burrow in desert

hp: Ground-running hunter — pursues prey rather than using a web. Carries egg sac. Desert habitat.

desc: Small flat running spider on bark and walls. Very fast. Flattened body for bark crevices.

fm.Color: Brown; flat; very fast on bark

hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Flattened body.

desc: Boxwood spider mite — tiny tetranychid mite on boxwood causing leaf bronzing, stippling, and defoliation. Pest of ornamental boxwood hedges throughout LA. Unlike red spider mites, does not produce heavy webbing. Feeds on leaf undersides. Treated with miticides or predatory mites.
hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. Preys on spider mite.

desc: Small dark gnaphosid named for dense facial setae. Fast nocturnal runner under rocks and debris. Like all gnaphosids, has flattened, tubular spinnerets. Hunts by pursuit rather than by web. Beneficial garden predator.
hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Tiny predatory arachnid with four-armed pincers (chelae). In leaf litter and soil. Hunts springtails and mites.
hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Four-armed.
